title: `podman run` bails out if you ^C a `git clone` running as non-root
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

I'm setting up a container for CI, and one thing CI needs to check is that build as non-root user isn't broken. What I stumbled upon is that if I log into a container as a non-root and run `git clone`, and then I <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+<kbd>c</kbd> that command, podman quits.

This does not happen if interrupting e.g. `sleep n` command or an infinite bash cycle, so might be something to do with network… I assume there are other ways to trigger the problem that I didn't stumble upon yet.

**Steps to reproduce the issue *(in terms of terminal commands)*:**

```haskell
 λ cat Dockerfile
FROM docker.io/library/ubuntu:bionic
RUN useradd user -m
RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y git
 λ podman build . -t test
STEP 1: FROM docker.io/library/ubuntu:bionic
STEP 2: RUN useradd user -m
--> Using cache 84ccd08ce7a1c8becf387bd755a7ff38f1d2c49b0b78802d901e06f97d91c4bf
--> 84ccd08ce7a
STEP 3: RUN apt-get update
--> Using cache 8352484b6995152c2c77a20e16567bfc8953a2ceb57319f72915fd2b39b4b5f3
--> 8352484b699
STEP 4: RUN apt-get install -y git
--> Using cache 3cbb45349cbd4e5f3ad2b80af9174ffae18e5634a720d243fc3edd9189fbc19d
STEP 5: COMMIT test
--> 3cbb45349cb
3cbb45349cbd4e5f3ad2b80af9174ffae18e5634a720d243fc3edd9189fbc19d
 λ podman run -it test su - user
$ git clone https://github.com/torvalds/linux
Cloning into 'linux'...
^C
$ %
 λ
```

**Describe the results you received:**

Interrupting the last command resulted in podman bailing out of container.

**Describe the results you expected:**

podman would not exit.

**Additional information you deem important:**

When podman exits, the exit code is `130`.

Running podman under gdb results in gdb reporting `Inferior 1 (process 87385) exited with code 0202]`

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
 λ podman --version
podman version 3.1.0
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

<details>


comments.body:
@Hi-Angel I don't think this is a `podman` issue. The `docker`'s behaviour is the same as the `podman` ones. I think that problem here is that `su` is not a good entrypoint (it won't forward SIGINIT as `/bin/sh` entrypoint does).

So I think you should switch user in `Dockerfile`
```Dockerfile
FROM docker.io/library/ubuntu:bionic
RUN useradd user -m
RUN apt-get update
RUN apt-get install -y git
USER user
```
and then use standard entrypoint `/bin/sh`.

> So I think you should switch user in `Dockerfile`
> 
> ```dockerfile
> FROM docker.io/library/ubuntu:bionic
> RUN useradd user -m
> RUN apt-get update
> RUN apt-get install -y git
> USER user
> ```
> 
> and then use standard entrypoint `/bin/sh`.

Thank you for the workaround, will do!

> @Hi-Angel I don't think this is a `podman` issue. The `docker`'s behaviour is the same as the `podman` ones. I think that problem here is that `su` is not a good entrypoint (it won't forward SIGINIT as `/bin/sh` entrypoint does).

On the other hand, it's not what `su - user` does out of container. Since the purpose of container is to virtualize a system, and a real system would not bail out of `su` upon pressing ^C while running certain command, I think it's fair to say that both `docker` and `podman` has a bug.

Besides, even if we consider it an intentional behavior, another point here is that podman prints no messages about why it exited.

I do not understand why `sleep n` exits as expected.
Only difference I see now is that `git` runs multiple processes:
```docker top naughty_pasteur
UID                 PID                 PPID                C                   STIME               TTY                 TIME                CMD
root                466635              466613              0                   18:22               pts/0               00:00:00            su - user
t1000               466673              466635              0                   18:22               pts/0               00:00:00            -su
t1000               466912              466673              2                   18:22               pts/0               00:00:00            git clone https://github.com/containers/podman
t1000               466913              466912              17                  18:22               pts/0               00:00:00            /usr/lib/git-core/git-remote-https origin https://github.com/containers/podman
t1000               466925              466913              7                   18:22               pts/0               00:00:00            /usr/lib/git-core/git fetch-pack --stateless-rpc --stdin --lock-pack --thin --check-self-contained-and-connected --cloning https://github.com/containers/podman/
t1000               466927              466925              0                   18:22               pts/0               00:00:00            /usr/lib/git-core/git index-pack --stdin -v --fix-thin --keep=fetch-pack 14 on 0a2d9ccb046d --check-self-contained-and-connected --pack_header=2,114524
```

>Forgot to say: even if it would be considered an intentional behavior, another point here is that podman prints no messages about why it exited.

At least it return errcode 130 -- SIGINT.
@Hi-Angel I tried this with Fedora, CentOS and Alpine (instead of Ubuntu) and the bug is not present. Couldn't be something wrong with `su` on Ubuntu?
Hmm, this is interesting, yeah, it might be! Thank you very much, I'll try taking a closer look at it tomorrow then.

So… I did a lot of experimentation, and I do think you are right in that it is purely a `su` bug on the older Ubuntu, and is not specific to virtualization.

Specifically, it seems the bug is that under some circumstances `su` probably tries to do something with PID `1`, but then it does not expect that `su` *is* the process with PID 1. The workaround is simply making sure `su - user` does not have PID of `1`. For example, replacing the command with `sh -c "su - user"` fixes the problem.

Although I'm curious, but I didn't manage to figure out what exactly it tries to do with PID 1, because whatever I do with debugging tools inside the container results in either of α) PID of `su` getting changed to `2` *(e.g. attaching `strace`)*, or β) in container exiting without leaving me any chance to see stacktrace or anything. Oh, well…
